llcFilesWith: City or town clerk in every city or town where the business maintains an office (there is no state-level DBA filing)
soleProprietorFilesWith: City or town clerk in every city or town where the business maintains an office, on the same business certificate
feeBasis: Each city or town sets its own fee, commonly in the $30 to $65 range (Boston charges $65). A business with offices in more than one municipality files, and pays, in each.
renewalRule: A business certificate is effective for four years and is renewed by filing again with the same city or town clerk.
protectionNote: A business certificate, known in Massachusetts as a d/b/a, is a municipal notice filing that identifies who is behind a business name; it grants no exclusive right. Because it is filed with the city or town clerk and not the Secretary of the Commonwealth, it is not part of the state entity-name screen, so it neither blocks nor is blocked by names in the Corporations Division records.
searchNote: Business certificates are held in each city or town clerk's records, not in the Secretary of the Commonwealth's business search, so a full check means contacting the clerk in every municipality where a business operates. There is no statewide index of d/b/a filings.
notes: Massachusetts keeps DBAs entirely at the municipal level. Every business, whether an LLC, corporation, or sole proprietor, that operates under a name other than its legal name files a business certificate with the city or town clerk in each municipality where it has an office (M.G.L. c. 110, § 5). Fees are set locally, the certificate lasts four years, and there is no newspaper-publication requirement and no statewide filing. Because these certificates sit outside the state records, they do not enter the LLC name distinguishability test.
